easy: We learned to see at long last. Partway through the millennium, we figured out how to shape and polish glass to see far and to see small, and we explored ancient languages to name our new tools of vision such as the telescope, microscope, spectroscope, spectrophotometer, spectroheliograph, and eventually television. We understood perspective, expanded our vision beyond natural limits, and explored both the vast universe and the microscopic world, discovering everything from quasars to viruses. Scientists like Anton van Leeuwenhoek observed microorganisms, while Isaac Newton deepened our understanding of light and knowledge. Over time, our vision extended into the fourth dimension as we learned to represent time through charts and diagrams. This idea was even imagined in literature by H. G. Wells through concepts like time travel, showing how human understanding of vision, space, and time has continuously evolved. Of the many outcomes of the 2020 Delhi Assembly election, one that stood out was the public emergence of Arvind Kejriwal as a Hanuman bhakt. In his victory speech, he thanked Lord Hanuman for blessing Delhi and stated that the mandate given to the Aam Aadmi Party marked the beginning of a "new form of politics" in India. He described this as "kaam ki rajneeti" (politics of work), where people vote based on development such as schools, hospitals, affordable healthcare, and electricity. While development remained the central theme, his campaign also included a strategic display of religious identity to balance the political narrative and reduce polarization. By presenting himself as a devout Hanuman bhakt and focusing on governance, he retained voter support and prevented a shift towards opposing narratives. This approach resulted in a decisive victory, with 62 out of 70 seats, and his speech reinforced both his development agenda and his religious positioning, expressing gratitude to Lord Hanuman and committing to continue serving the people of Delhi.

When the universe was young, the sky was filled with planets, stars, stardust, and countless rocks. Among them was one special rock, unlike any other. She was kind and cheerful, always floating near a big blue planet. At times, when light touched her surface, she glowed a brilliant green, almost appearing as something more than just a rock. As time passed, she noticed distant planets and often wondered what it would be like to visit them. One day, driven by curiosity, she decided to explore. At first unsure how to move, she began to rock back and forth, then spin, until she was finally soaring through space. As she left, the big blue planet grew sad, and clouds gathered, bringing rain. Her journey was not easy at first, but slowly she learned to guide herself. She traveled across space, discovering planets of all kinds some covered in endless water, others dry with sandy lands, some icy cold, and even one that shimmered with jewels and gold. Each planet was unique and more beautiful than the last, making it difficult for her to choose where to go or where to stay. She continued traveling, afraid of missing any wonder, until one day she returned to the big blue planet she once knew. But now it looked brighter and more beautiful than ever before. Confused, she wondered why. The planet gently told her that it had grown brighter because of her. Though she thought she was just a rock, the planet explained that she had become something more a shining shooting star whose light could brighten any world. At last, she understood that her light did not depend on where she went it came from within her. And so, as she paused beside the glowing blue planet, she finally knew what she wanted to do.

Mr. Vacca walked to the door and then into the hallway and looked for anyone who might be lost or running a few minutes late. He noticed some people had set up display tables for a woodworkers art exhibit. There were many items already on the tables including decorative bowls, toy trains and even some musical instruments. When he turned to go back into the room, he accidentally knocked a beautiful wooden ink pen off the table. Thats okay, honey, said a very large woman standing on the other side of the table. She was wearing blue jean overalls covering a bright pink t-shirt. You can keep it. I didnt even make it. My pappy said that he found it when he was working on the Panama Canal. Its made from some kind of illegal rosewood. That thing has given me nothing but a headache. I cant even sell it, and I am tired of lugging it around. You’ll be doing me a favor by taking it, darling.

Mr. Vacca handed out the papers that he brought for the planned activity. As you can see, I have prepared examples of famous opening sentences. What I would like for you to do is write a similar sentence that you would use if you were going to write a novel about your life and had the chance to live it all over again. You can begin now. A few minutes passed by when Jimmy Necot spoke up, Excuse me, Mr. Vacca. I seem to have lost my pen. Do you have one that I could borrow? Mr. Vacca reached into his shirt pocket and handed Jimmy the pen that he had picked up earlier in the hallway. When Jimmy started to write his opening sentence, something very strange happened.

The black ink that made the letters on the white paper began to spread like drops of iodine in a bucket of ammonia. It diffused to the edge of the paper, across the floor and finally to the baseboards and up the walls. The ink that trailed up the walls formed cracks that opened wider and wider. Everyone, except Jimmy, was shocked to see the fantasy world form right before their eyes. Jimmy wrote about his childhood dream of being a racecar driver. The walls of the library basement disappeared and the scene opened up to a huge NASCAR race. Mr. Vacca reached up and felt headphones on his ears and looked at the microphone placed in front of him. He was an announcer on a trackside tower. The racecars roared past in a thunderous symphony and someone beside him elbowed him in the side.
medium: The Marmabill has lost her nest and sets out on a brave journey through the rainforest to find it again. Along the way, she meets a variety of fantastical creatures, including gentle wugs, helpful tankadiggies, and some less friendly ones like mischievous key-keys and cranky fluthers. Her adventure takes her from lush green treetops to mysterious glowing caves, where she faces challenges and discovers new experiences. As she travels, the Marmabill begins to understand the true meaning of home. What starts as a search for her lost nest turns into a deeper journey of self-discovery, making her realize that home may be something more special than she first imagined. With vivid illustrations by Tiffany Turrill and a charming story by Daniel Errico, this delightful tale is filled with playful rhymes and imaginative creatures, making it an enjoyable read for both children and adults. A gnarble left his cozy bed along the ocean floor. He dreamt about a place that he had never seen before. He headed to the surface for a glimpse of sun and sky. A trip so long and perilous, he'd be the first to try. The Journey of the Noble Gnarble is a number one best-selling ebook that has been adapted into a hardcover book and play. Through rhyming verse and engaging illustrations, the Noble Gnarble teaches kids about perseverance and determination in the face of obstacles. Dream Big!

Long ago, there was no night. The Sun and the Moon were best friends who shared the sky together all the time. The Sun shone brightly over the land, while the Moon watched over the quiet oceans. But life was not easy for the animals. Elephant, who lived on the plains, suffered under the constant heat. No matter what he tried, he could not cool down. One day, he called out to the Sun, asking for relief, but the Sun did not listen. In the forest, Owl struggled as well. She could not hunt in the bright daylight, and every attempt to catch food failed. She too called out to the Sun, but again, there was no response. By the ocean, Baby Turtle faced his own problem. Without the Moon's tides, he could not reach the sea. He pleaded with the Moon to help, and this time, she listened. Hearing the animals' struggles, the Moon spoke to the Sun, and together they decided to help. They made a plan to share the sky—when the Sun would rise, the Moon would set, bringing daylight, and when the Moon would rise, the Sun would set, bringing cool darkness and tides. However, they needed someone to tell them when to switch. The animals refused one by one, until a small, unnoticed creature named Dullfly was chosen. At first, he was afraid, saying he was too small for such an important task. But the Sun gave him a special gift, making him glow with light. From that day on, he became Firefly. Firefly took on the responsibility of signaling dawn and dusk, guiding the Sun and Moon in their turns. And so, night and day were created, bringing balance to the world. Even today, if you see a firefly glowing at dawn or dusk, remember that it is helping the Sun and Moon keep their promise.

Emails remain the main tool of communication in professional life. A good email should be short, polite, and clear, usually between 50 words. Greetings like Hello, closings like Thank you, and sign-offs like Regards help maintain respect. Using ALL CAPS, for example PLEASE REPLY, feels rude or aggressive. Attachments should be small in size, not more than 25MB, unless compressed. Professionals often check emails two times a day, but too many notifications can reduce focus. A well-written email not only shares information but also builds trust and long-term relationships. In todays world, good email habits are as valuable as speaking skills.

Before there were seasons, the cricket could fly. He was faster than a bumblebee, higher than a falcon, and even fancier than a flying marmaduck. He had traveled all over the world East, West, and South but he had never flown North. The ant warned him not to go, saying it was too cold for both ants and crickets. But the cricket did not listen, because crickets do whatever they want. That very night, he set off for the North, reaching it quickly with his fast and graceful flying. As he landed near a pine tree at sunset, he saw snow everywhere on the trees, the ground, and the mountains. A moose slowly approached and advised him to bundle up before nightfall. Still, the cricket ignored the warning and flew up into a tree to rest. Soon, an owl joined him on the branch and gently warned him again to find proper shelter from the cold night.

I must warn you this book is truly atrocious! The cover is covered in mud, some pages are damaged, and even the words seem out of order. Still, the story begins in a very unusual kingdom ruled by an equally atrocious king whose laws made no sense. He even declared that Tuesday did not exist, replacing it with strange alternatives like "second Monday" or "first Wednesday." The king's daughter, an equally troublesome princess, was nothing like the polite royals one would expect. She ignored her lessons, never cared for her appearance, and loved causing mischief by setting traps. One day, she mysteriously disappeared, leaving behind a confusing note. To find her, the king sent the greatest hero in the land though he, too, was quite unusual. Despite being easily frightened, he had once defeated a few small creatures, which somehow earned him his reputation. His search led him to a kind-hearted villain, a tidy and gentle dragon, and finally to the queen, whose treasures had also gone missing. Each clue only deepened the mystery. Following muddy footprints, they eventually discovered the truth in the tallest tower of the castle. There sat the princess, surrounded by all the stolen treasures. She admitted that she had taken everything simply to avoid her writing lessons. The queen made her return everything and apologize, and from that day on, the princess was required to attend her lessons regularly. Though she started as an atrocious student, she gradually improved. Meanwhile, the others returned to their unusual lives, and the entire strange adventure was recorded in one of the queen's muddy books. And so, as promised, this was indeed an atrocious tale but perhaps one day, you might write a better one!

Before words existed as we know them, a boy named Yes lived in a small village in a small kingdom. He was good at everything the smartest, the most capable, and the most liked person in the village. Yes had a brother named No, who was jealous of him because he was not good at much. Whenever the villagers asked No for help, he refused, as he did not like people. In contrast, whenever someone asked Yes for help, he gladly agreed, secretly enjoying how much it annoyed his brother. One day, their father, Okay, went on a long journey and left the two brothers in charge of the animals. Yes took great care of them the great guck, the icks, and the three-toed yock (very old animals that may be known by different names today). However, No had no interest in such responsibilities. Instead of helping, he wandered off to the lake and spent his time throwing rocks into the water.
hard: A few days after their father left, Yes asked his brother No to watch the three-toed yock while he went to find food. However, instead of helping, No lay down, closed his eyes, and forgot about the yock. Meanwhile, the King often traveled along a nearby road. By chance, the three-toed yock wandered onto that very road just as the King's carriage was passing. The driver had to swerve to avoid hitting it, causing the King to be thrown from the carriage and injured. Angered, the King demanded an explanation from the villagers. As always, Yes told the truth and explained everything. After thinking for a moment, the King decided that both Yes and No would serve as his personal assistants in the castle as punishment for what became known as the worst three-toed yock and carriage accident in the kingdom's history. Soon, the King realized that only Yes was helpful. No proved to be completely unreliable anything he was asked to bring would end up lost, broken, or eaten. The King became convinced that No was the worst servant in the world. One of their most important duties was to help prepare for a grand feast the King was hosting that night. Cooks from across the kingdom arrived at the castle gates, offering their finest dishes. Since the King could not collect the food himself, he asked Yes and No to do it. When the first cook arrived and announced a delicious ugberry pie for the banquet, the King was delighted. Wanting to ensure nothing went wrong, he immediately called for Yes and asked him to bring the pie.

Warren was just an ordinary wolf pup until the full moon revealed something extraordinary. While other wolves became wilder under its glow, Warren transformed into something completely different: a polite young boy. For Warren was, quite unusually, a Wereboy. In the forest, wolves behaved as wolves do howling at the moon, rolling in the dirt, and living freely without manners or rules. But Warren was not like them. He avoided howling, ran back to his cave at night, and there, under the bright full moon, he would change. His fur disappeared, his paws became hands, his snout shrank, and he stood upright like a human child. He even wore clothes and behaved with surprising politeness. While in this form, Warren preferred sandwiches without crusts, cleaned his cave, followed routines, and even chose to sleep properly instead of on the ground. By morning, he would return to being a wolf pup again, and his family kept his secret safe. However, during a grand celebration for Sinclair, the eldest wolf, Warren's absence raised suspicion. When the pack came to investigate, they discovered his transformation and were frightened, calling him a "Wereboy." But Warren remained calm, quietly reading, even as the others reacted with fear. His parents reassured the pack, explaining that change is natural and that Warren was still the same at heart only more than what they expected. To settle the matter, Sinclair proposed a test: all true wolves must howl at the moon. When Warren stepped forward, he did something new. Instead of howling, he sang a beautiful, heartfelt song that silenced the entire pack. One by one, they listened, moved by something they had never heard before. As morning came and Warren changed back into a wolf, the pack finally saw him differently not just as a wolf or a boy, but as something unique and special, both at once, and more than either alone.

Dennis didn't feel like sleeping. For as long as he could remember, he had gone to bed at the same time every night, and he was tired not sleepy, but sick and tired of following that routine. So one night, when fall was ending but winter had not yet begun, he decided to stay awake. What Dennis didn't realize was how difficult it is to stay awake when your body needs sleep. He tried everything he could think of. He counted wolves, hoping it would keep him alert, but it only made him more tired. He then suspected that it was the counting itself, not the animal, that caused sleepiness. He tried holding his eyelids open, which was both difficult and strange-looking. He even attempted solving math problems in his head, but thinking of problems was just as boring as solving them. Just as he was about to give up, he heard a faint squeak from his hamster, Charlie. When he checked the cage, it was empty. Following the sound, he found Charlie on the floor near the dresser, squeaking as small objects paperclips, paper balls, and even LEGO pieces were being thrown at him. Curious, Dennis picked up Charlie and looked behind the dresser. What he saw made him question whether he had accidentally fallen asleep it was a gnome. About the same size as Charlie, the gnome wore a yellow shirt, green pants, and a pointy hat. Regardless of what it was, it was clearly being rude to his pet. Dennis asked why the gnome was throwing things at Charlie, but the gnome insisted the hamster was a "vicious beast" and had started it. Charlie squeaked in protest. Confused and slightly annoyed, Dennis asked why the gnome was in his room. The gnome replied that it was "highly classified information." When Dennis corrected him, saying his name wasn't "human" but Dennis, the gnome responded that he already knew his name and reminded him that gnomes have names too. After a brief and awkward silence during which Charlie fell asleep in Dennis's hands Dennis finally asked, "Well then, what is your name?"

"Michael the Marvelous," king of the land and son of the famous "Robert the Grand," celebrated his thirty-second birthday with a grand feast. Guests from far and wide filled the castle princes, princesses, and nobles alike. A towering seven-foot cake amazed the crowd, while music, dancing, and laughter filled the night. Just as the Queen finished her heartfelt toast, a dark cloud appeared, and from it emerged an old, ragged man accompanied by a golden-beaked duck. The strange visitor approached the King and offered him a gift a magical duck that could grant wishes. Before anyone could question him, the old man vanished into the cloud, leaving the duck behind. Though confused, the King continued the celebration. The duck sat quietly beside him until, in a moment of joy, the King made a careless wish that the night would never end. The duck's beak glowed blue, granting the wish, and then it flew away. But the wish came with terrible consequences. Morning never arrived again. Darkness covered the kingdom, crops began to wither, and life grew difficult under endless night. Realizing his mistake, the King offered great rewards to anyone who could find the old man and undo the curse. Many searched, but no one succeeded. Far away in a forest stood a small cabin, where the old man revealed to be a wizard named Sonto the Bad lived in exile. Beside him sat the magical duck and a locked box. Inside that box, secured by magic, was the sun itself hidden away, and only magic could bring it back.

As weeks turned into months, the sun never returned, and the kingdom remained in darkness. The people grew restless and unhappy, and at last, King Michael could bear it no longer. Determined to fix his mistake, he set out alone on horseback something kings rarely did. Ignoring the council's advice to travel east, he rode west, facing dangerous creatures along the way. Despite the dangers, he bravely continued his journey. One evening, while resting, he fell asleep and was awakened by a noise. Nearby, he discovered a small cabin. Peering inside, he found the old wizard, Sonto the Bad. Without hesitation, the King burst in and demanded that the wizard undo the curse and return the sun. Sonto refused but offered a challenge instead. Inside a locked box, he said, lay the magic needed to restore the sun. To open it, the King had to solve three riddles. If he failed, he would lose his kingdom. Accepting the challenge, King Michael answered each riddle with wisdom and calm. He solved all three correctly, proving his intelligence and worth. With that, the box opened, releasing a glowing light that rose into the sky and became the sun, finally ending the endless night. The King returned to his castle, where a grand celebration was held in his honor. The wizard was punished but later given simple duties, and peace was restored to the kingdom. However, the magical duck was never seen again. And so, it is said if you ever come across such a duck, be careful what you wish for.

Cybersecurity protects digital data from hackers, who attempt millions of attacks daily. In 2024, over 30M records were leaked due to phishing and brute force methods. Phishing emails trick people into sharing passwords, while brute force can test 1,000+ passwords per second. Strong encryption methods like AES-256 make stolen data nearly impossible to break without a key. Still, 90% of breaches occur due to human error, such as weak passwords or ignored warnings. Companies spend billions of dollars each year to secure systems, but personal safety habits matter too. As technology grows, cybersecurity becomes one of the most critical fields in the world.

excel_practical_tasks.description: Apply the UPPER, LOWER, and PROPER functions to the data in column A of the Function sheet.
Sort column A in ascending order on the Sort sheet.
Replace 'Gentleman' with 'Doctor' in column A on the Replace sheet.
Concatenate columns A and B into column C on the Concatenate sheet.
Calculate the Sum, Average, Maximum, and Minimum values of column A in cells B2, C2, D2, and E2 respectively on the Sum & Average sheet.
Insert one row above Row 9, enter the data 'Joseph, 26, Doctor', and delete Column D.
Trim the text in column A, place the trimmed results in column B, and calculate Column B lengths in column C on the Trim & Length sheet.
Apply the LEFT and RIGHT functions to extract first letter from column A on the Text Functions sheet.
Count the total number of cells that are not empty in column A place the result in cell B2 on the Count Sheet.
Identify and remove duplicate values in column A using conditional formatting on the Duplicates sheet.
